_source: EXIF via macOS mdls, extracted 2026-07-15. All 26 photos carry both timestamp and GPS.
_timezone: ts_mdt is America/Denver (MDT, UTC-6) — local Montana time during the trip.

_photographer_attribution.basis: Two distinct cameras in the set. Mapped to anglers via the session-3 transcript, twice independently.
_photographer_attribution.iPhone 17 Pro: thorne — Thorne says 'my last picture... Saturday, 1:30' -> IMG_1024 @ 13:30:47 Sat, iPhone 17 Pro. And 'first one is cut... 9:51' -> IMG_0983 @ 09:51:17, iPhone 17 Pro.
_photographer_attribution.caveat: photographer != angler. Thorne photographed Luyen's fish and vice versa. 'angler' is a separate field and is NOT yet populated.

_unresolved: species/angler/fly are null pending a photo-by-photo pass against the transcripts.
The Missouri float line CANNOT be derived from these photos — only 2-3 points per day. Need put-in/take-out from Luyen, then snap to river centerline.
Sat and Sun clusters are ~2km apart on the creek. Both assumed DePuy's; needs confirmation.

moments.caption: Friday, 2:55 PM — barely off the plane and already walking the grass at DePuy's. Thorne scouts past the old wooden buildings on the first stop of the trip.
moments.notable: Per Luyen this stop is likely the visit to the little DePuy's fly shop that was closed when they arrived — the dark wooden building behind Thorne is plausibly that shop, though the frame doesn't confirm it. Image needs a 90-degree rotation before display. Timestamp (2:55 PM Fri) sits slightly earlier than the 'late afternoon' arrival recalled in session 1.

moments.caption: Arrival-day dividend: the West Boulder gave up exactly one fish that Friday evening, and this rainbow was it — Thorne's, netted at 5:53 PM while Luyen went fishless.
moments.notable: The cap literally reads 'WHERE'S THE FISH?' — answered, in this frame. Same green vest and sheepskin fly patch as IMG_1612, useful for identity continuity across days.

moments.caption: Saturday, 8:47 AM at DePuy's — rod out over the glass, weed beds swaying underneath, about an hour before the morning's first fish came to hand.
moments.notable: Timestamped 8:47 AM Saturday — the calm-before shot preceding the 9:42/9:51/10:01 fish sequence at nearly the same GPS point. Needs a 90-degree rotation before display. The water clarity and weed beds read unmistakably as spring creek.

moments.caption: Thorne's Saturday-morning cutthroat at DePuy's, 9:42 AM — slash-jawed and copper-bellied, one of the three he took that morning, all on PMDs. Nine minutes later Luyen answered with a cutthroat of his own.
moments.notable: Strongest catch photo in this batch — direct sun, full fish broadside, genuine smile, blue sky and creek behind. The sheepskin patch on his vest carries several flies. This cutthroat may be the 'cut' in Thorne's hit-for-the-cycle run, though the transcript leaves the cycle's exact day ambiguous.

moments.caption: First one is a cutthroat, 9:51. Luyen opens Saturday morning at DePuy's with a Yellowstone cutthroat — bamboo rod and click reel tucked under his arm, spots crowding the tail, that telltale orange under the jaw.
moments.notable: The bamboo rod and silver click reel are prominently visible resting across the net hoop — a nice gear detail. A vehicle and fence line are visible on the road atop the bank behind him.

moments.caption: One minute before the hero shot: the slab rests in Luyen's net at DePuy's, trailing a strand of creek weed, while the rusty spinner's work is done for the moment.
moments.notable: Strands of green aquatic weed cling to the fish — same weedy spring-creek detail the anglers remarked on for another catch. The wooden net handle carries a maker's stamp. Ambiguity worth flagging: the transcript's 'other one in the net... riffle corner' line could refer to this frame or to FullSizeRender.jpeg (10:49 AM, also Thorne's camera).

moments.caption: The slab. Ten minutes after the cutthroat, Luyen cradles a really big, thick DePuy's rainbow — probably fooled by the same rusty spinner that would carry the whole day.
moments.notable: The fish's girth is striking relative to the hands holding it, which sells the 'slab' nickname. Both of Luyen's hands are in frame, meaning Thorne shot this close over his shoulder.

moments.caption: Two in rapid succession: Luyen with the Saturday-morning slab, a rainbow nearly as long as the frame, moments before it went back into DePuy's Spring Creek.
moments.notable: Shot six seconds after IMG_1003 — same fish, pulled back to get Luyen's face in frame. The fish spans nearly the full width of the photo, which makes its size legible in a way the close-up doesn't.

moments.caption: Buttery flanks and haloed spots over a bed of spring-creek weed — a DePuy's brown settles in the net, late Saturday morning. If the reconstruction holds, this is the fish that completed Thorne's cut-bow-brown cycle.
moments.notable: The only net-in-water fish shot in this batch. The clear water shows the weed beds DePuy's browns tuck into. Angler attribution is the one open question here — transcript supports both Thorne (his Saturday cycle brown) and Luyen ('the other one in the net, I think was my fish'), and the visual species (brown, not cut) slightly undercuts Luyen's own recollection.

moments.caption: Rod bent, line tight — Thorne hooked up on DePuy's late Saturday morning, with the mountains of Paradise Valley standing watch.
moments.notable: The only frame in this batch of an angler actively fighting a fish — the full arc of the rod against open sky makes it a natural hero image. The clear-rubber net on his hip matches the net in FullSizeRender.jpeg, taken 51 minutes earlier on his phone. No landed fish is visible, so this hookup can't be tied to a specific catch.

moments.caption: Saturday, high noon at DePuy's: two grins, a shoulder patch full of flies, and one cap asking the eternal question — WTF, Where's the Fish?
moments.notable: Luyen's mirrored lenses reflect his own outstretched arm holding the phone — confirming this is a selfie on his iPhone 17 Pro Max. Thorne's 'WTF — Where's the Fish?' cap is a gift of a detail. The wool patch on Thorne's vest has flies drying in it. This is the best 'the two of them together' frame for the site.

moments.caption: Number five of the morning? Luyen steadies a DePuy's rainbow at the waterline before letting it go — the rusty spinner was the ticket, and the bamboo rod never left his arm.
moments.notable: Beautiful release-moment framing — fish half in the water, not a grip-and-grin, which suits the sight-fishing ethos of the trip. The click reel and cork grip are clearly visible for gear-heads. At 12:14 PM this is the last catch photo of Saturday morning before the 1:30 wrap-up shots.

moments.caption: Last frame before the midday nap — Luyen at the picnic-table pullout on DePuy's, Saturday 1:30, the Absarokas doing their thing behind him.
moments.notable: The culvert visible at right matches Thorne's later map-pin comment 'So that is at a culvert.' This is the picnic-table parking spot they returned to both DePuy's days.

moments.caption: Thorne's turn at the picnic-table spot, forty-six seconds later — sandals on, day's fishing done, Saturday 1:31 before the drive back for a nap.
moments.notable: Taken with Thorne's own phone even though Thorne is the subject — evidence they handed one phone back and forth for this pair of portraits. Sandals, not waders: they were off the water for the midday break.

moments.caption: The riffle-corner rainbow, Exhibit A — off the outside bend where the riffle dumps into the pool, the red-bodied spinner still in its lip.
moments.notable: Species per Luyen's own account (previously logged cutbow). The red-bodied fly visible in its lip is Jamie's 1-in-7 spinner.

moments.caption: "It's cool that it's got the weed there" — Thorne brings his Sunday-morning brown to the net on DePuy's, rod high, duckweed and all.
moments.notable: This is plausibly the netting moment of the brown that completed Thorne's 'hit for the cycle' morning (cutthroat, rainbow, brown). Weed is visibly hanging from the net — the detail Thorne loved about the brown-trout photo.

moments.caption: Thorne with the 'bow' leg of his cycle — rainbow in hand, rod in teeth, DePuy's on a Sunday morning.
moments.notable: The rod held in his teeth and the fly-studded wool patch on the vest make this the most character-rich catch photo of the set; the wooden net visible underwater matches the net in other DePuy's shots.

moments.caption: PMD duns on every single blade of grass — the bank at DePuy's, Sunday midday.
moments.notable: This is the exact photo Luyen describes taking in the debrief — the transcript line maps one-to-one. Taken on Luyen's phone Sun 12:10 PM at DePuy's. Explains what the trout were eating that morning (Thorne's day-1 fish all came on PMDs).

moments.caption: The cut from the riffle corner, resting in the net — Luyen's fish, DePuy's day two.
moments.notable: The only in-the-net fish photo in this batch — it is almost certainly 'the other one in the net' the anglers debate in session 3, which they resolve as Luyen's cutthroat.

moments.caption: The last fish of DePuy's, and Thorne's most memorable — a big brown that sat under the duckweed mat with just its snout up, sipping. He thought it was a small fish. It was not.
moments.notable: This is the brown that completed Thorne's cut-bow-brown 'cycle' day at DePuy's, and the release-in-the-water framing with the weed bed is the detail both anglers remembered ('it's got the weed there'). Taken Sun 1:10 PM.

moments.caption: Welcome to the Missouri — Thorne under a cloud of bugs at Craig, Sunday evening, before a single cast.
moments.notable: This matches the known non-fish photo Luyen described as 'a Trico cloud above Thorne's head at the Missouri launch.' EXIF puts it at Craig, Sun 8:01 PM — the evening they arrived at the Missouri after leaving DePuy's, foreshadowing the Trico clouds they'd float through at Holter the next morning. Image needs a 90-degree rotation for display.

moments.caption: First light on the Missouri, day one at Holter: Tricos in clouds thick enough to catch the sun, a lone figure already in the water. Luyen said he'd never seen anything like it.
moments.notable: The 'snow' filling the sky is thousands of individual backlit Tricos, not sensor dust — a genuinely unusual capture. A drift boat and a second distant angler are visible mid-river, confirming the 'massive numbers of boats' at the dam. Slight green lens flare lower left.

moments.caption: Fourteen minutes into the first Missouri morning: Thorne with the rainbow that ate a tiny Trico on the far bank, right where they crossed below Holter Dam.
moments.notable: File is rotated 90 degrees — needs orientation correction before display. The amber wool fly-drying patch on Thorne's vest and the mirror-calm reflection of his silhouette are nice details; this is the only confirmed Thorne fish photo from the Missouri days in this batch.

moments.caption: Luyen didn't land any of the early sippers, but stuck a nice rainbow on the hopper-dropper once the drift continued.
moments.notable: Species corrected to rainbow per Luyen (agents had guessed cutthroat from an apparent jaw slash; angler overrules).

moments.caption: Back at Monday's bank on the last morning, and this time it paid: Luyen slips his long-cast Trico rainbow home. "I love the one I caught in the morning."
moments.notable: GPS puts this within ~30 m of IMG_1728 from the previous morning — visual proof they returned to the same 'prime timey' water. Luyen is in a plaid shirt rather than his usual sun hoodie; his rod's green-marbled reel seat matches the rod laid out in IMG_1831.

moments.caption: The last Missouri fish and the one Luyen loved: a big, deep-bodied rainbow from the inside braid of the island, laid beside the five-weight for a moment before going back.
moments.notable: The rod blank is legible — 'ALBRIGHT EXR-FW / 590-4 / #5 Line 9'0" / 2.8 oz' — a concrete gear fact for the site, and it visually ties this fish to Luyen via the matching reel in IMG_1826. Tiny purple speedwell flowers in the weeds are a lovely detail.

moments.caption: Thorne at the Wolf Creek bridge with a deep-bodied Missouri rainbow, held straight at the lens — wool patch loaded, WTF cap on duty. Shot on a real camera, no less.
moments.notable: Third camera in the set: a Nikon Z6 III. Tuesday 9:52 AM — slots between the morning riser bank and the midday island braid.

moments.caption: Buzz's posted hours, a masterpiece of the genre: “Open most days about 8 or 9… somedays we aren't here at all… except when I'm someplace else, but I should be here then too.” On this day: someplace else.
moments.notable: Timestamp corrected +2h for export shift; 4 min before the walking shot.

moments.caption: Thorne checks the Fisherman's Hut — carved trout over the door, nobody home there either.
W Boulder Rd, one mile — golden hour, a retired flatbed, and the road out after the evening's one fish.
moments.notable: Sign in frame corroborates the West Boulder ruling.

moments.caption: The scene of the crime, photographed after the fact — the lower creek at dusk, weed channels and glass. The fish are in there, smug.
moments.notable: No GPS on this frame; time places it mid-evening-session.

moments.caption: The road to the honey hole runs through the herd. One local, unimpressed. 8:52 PM.
moments.notable: GPS pins the evening session to the lower creek — corroborates Luyen's account.

moments.caption: Exhibit: 104° at 4:03 PM, northbound on US-287 to Craig. Circled for the record by the driver's counsel.
moments.notable: No EXIF GPS (edited copy); time from the dashboard clock itself.

moments.caption: Mid-drift, day one: a bald eagle and a juvenile share a ponderosa above the river. The competition, scouting.
moments.notable: New mid-float location — fills the gap between the midday cut and the two-island water.

_analysis: species/angler/captions merged 2026-07-16 from 6-agent visual pass cross-referenced against transcripts. Attribution basis in each agent report; anchor-calibrated.
_export_shift_note: Second photo batch (2026-07-17) carries a -2h timestamp shift from export; corrected here. Verified via burst neighbors of anchored originals.
_cycle_ruling: Cycle confirmed as SUNDAY (day two) per Luyen 2026-07-18, overruling the earlier Saturday reading. Bow = m17 (10:09 rod-in-teeth rainbow).
